coredata.dc:title: Carbon spheres derived nano graphitic flakes with excellent photoluminescence in organic solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Panickar, Radhika
Sobhan, C.B.
Chakravorti, Sivaji
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) synthesized carbon spheres (CS) were used to produce Nano Graphitic Flakes (NGFs) in different organic solvents with excellent photoluminescence properties. It was observed that simple manual shaking of CS in organic solvents results in liquid-phase exfoliation of CS in different organic solvents and the centrifuged supernatant exhibited excellent photoluminescence. This facile approach can give a large yield of NGFs in organic solvents such as Acetone, Methanol, Ethanol, DMF, DMSO, Toluene, isopropyl alcohol, Ethylene glycol (EG), Styrene, and Xylene. The mechanism of carbon dot formation and optical properties are discussed here.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon spheres
Carbon dots
Chemical vapor deposition
Graphitic flakes
Photoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of NGFs synthesis in organic solvents.

a) SEM image b) TEM image of the CS with the size 200–350 nm from CVD synthesis c) HR-TEM image of CS showing the NGFs attached to the surface of the sphere and d) schematic representation of the formation mechanism of CS.
a-b) SEM image of the CS residue obtained after the centrifuge process with significant size reduction.

a) UV absorption spectrum obtained for different organic solvents. The Photoluminescence spectrum of the supernatant obtained from various solvents b) acetone, c) methanol, d) ethanol, e) isopropyl alcohol, f) EG, g) styrene, h) xylene, i) toluene, j) DMSO, k) DMF.
Characterization of NGFs in DMF a) & b) HR-TEM image and inset of (b) give the SAED pattern. XPS survey spectra c) high-resolution XPS d) C1s, e) N1s, and f) O1s.
Lists the different organic solvents with their boiling points and surface tension values.

head.ce:title: Carbon spheres derived nano graphitic flakes with excellent photoluminescence in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Technology, Calicut 673 601, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institute of Technology, Calicut, India 673 601

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Calicut 673 601, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Calicut, India 673 601

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Institute of Technology, Calicut 673 601, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) synthesized carbon spheres (CS) were used to produce Nano Graphitic Flakes (NGFs) in different organic solvents with excellent photoluminescence properties. It was observed that simple manual shaking of CS in organic solvents results in liquid-phase exfoliation of CS in different organic solvents and the centrifuged supernatant exhibited excellent photoluminescence. This facile approach can give a large yield of NGFs in organic solvents such as Acetone, Methanol, Ethanol, DMF, DMSO, Toluene, isopropyl alcohol, Ethylene glycol (EG), Styrene, and Xylene. The mechanism of carbon dot formation and optical properties are discussed here.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL) is one of the most exciting properties in quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) observed due to their tiny particle sizes (<10 nm) and the functional groups attached to their surfaces [1,2]. PL is a property in which a material emits light by absorbing light. Along with this remarkable PL property, they possess unique advantages like superior photostability, high biocompatibility, lower toxicity, tunable PL properties, and ease of biomolecular functionalization [3–5]. The nature of these CDs and QDs is determined by their structure which depends on the method of synthesis and precursors used. Usually, CDs are amorphous or nanocrystalline that lack a definite shape resulting in edge defects with a variety of functional groups and dangling bonds attached which significantly affect the PL properties [6].

CDs have special features with excitation wavelength dependent and independent fluorescence with interesting colors that range from ultraviolet (UV) to near infrared (NIR) [7]. QDs and CDs have large prospects in bioimaging, drug delivery, and sensing biomolecules for their superior minimal toxicity, biocompatibility, and biological properties. Unlike the well-defined arrangements of carbon atoms in other carbon allotropes these carbon nanostructures are represented as combinations of polycyclic clusters with sp2 coordinated carbon atoms and aggregates of amorphous sp3 coordinated clusters [3,8]. Numerous synthesis pathways, encompassing both top-down and bottom-up techniques, have been documented for the synthesis of diverse QDs and CDs [9,10].

In the top-down approach of CDs and QDs synthesis, a bulk carbonic material gets decomposed to form tiny fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. Carbon nanotubes (CNT), carbon fibers, carbon nanoribbons, graphite powder, carbon black, and candle soot have been reported on the top-down synthesis of CDs and QDs [11,12]. However, the synthesis of CDs using carbon spheres and the formation mechanism is not yet registered. Numerous works have been reported on the synthesis and potential applications of CS owing to the high yield and feasible method of its synthesis [13–16]. In the present work, other than CDs and QDs, a facile method to synthesize NGF of 2–10 nm size in different organic solvents with excellent PL properties and high yield is reported for possible future advanced applications. A top-down approach of synthesis using CVD-synthesized CS as carbon precursors was used for the preparation of NGFs in organic solvents. Unlike dot-like structures seen in CDs and QDs, the NGFs are curly flake-like structures. Organic solvents: Acetone, Methanol, Ethanol, Dimethylformamide (DMF), Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), Toluene, Isopropyl alcohol, Ethylene glycol (EG), Styrene, and Xylene were investigated for the formation of NGFs using a simple manual shaking method.

A cost-effective and straightforward thermal CVD setup was used to synthesize CS under argon (Ar) as the carrier gas, and acetylene (C2H2), and hydrogen (H2) as the reactant gases. Relative uniform CS of size 200–350 nm were synthesized at an optimized deposition temperature of 950 °C in atmospheric pressure. A detailed deposition procedure and the effect of reactant gases during CS synthesis have been reported in our previous publication [17]. Fig. 2(a-b) shows the SEM and TEM images of the CS synthesized from a thermal CVD setup of size 200–350 nm.

The synthesis of NGFs was achieved by adding CS in organic solvents, followed by manual shaking. Organic solvents with low and high boiling points were selected for the test. Fig. 1 illustrates the method of synthesis of NGFs in different organic solvents. Table 1 gives the boiling points and surface tension of the different organic solvents used for this study. The organic solvents were taken in 10 ml bottles, and 5 mg of freshly prepared CS was added to each solvent. After manual shaking for a few seconds, the colorless solvents become pale yellow. The prepared solvents were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min, and the obtained supernatant was exposed to UV light, which exhibited excellent PL properties. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of the NGF formation in different organic solvents with excellent PL properties.

The structural and morphology analysis of the CS and the black residue after manual shaking is imaged using a Hitachi SU6600-Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) of resolution 1.2 nm with an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. A High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) with model JEM2100, JOEL Ltd. of resolution 0.24 nm was used to take SAED patterns and images of the CS and NGFs after the exfoliation process. The surface chemistry of the NGFs in DMF was analyzed using an X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), PHI 5000 VersaProbe II. ULVAC-PHI Inc., USA equipped with 200 μm, 15 KV monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray source of energy 1486.6 eV. The survey scans were recorded using a 50 W X-ray source and a pass energy of 187.85 eV.

To better understand the exfoliation of CS in organic solvents, the formation mechanism of CS must be known. The schematic representation of the CS formation mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 2 (d). Kang and Wang [18] reported that during the CVD process, the formation of CS was contributed by the pentagonal, hexagonal, and heptagonal carbon rings to accommodate the curvature of the CS.

It is believed that the spiral shell is nucleated from a pentagonal carbon ring. During the CVD deposition process, numerous pentagonal and heptagonal carbon rings accumulate on these quasi-icosahedral shells as shown in Fig. 2(d). The paired pentagonal-heptagonal carbon rings act as nucleation sites for atom-thick nanographitic flakes. The pentagonal and heptagonal carbon rings force the hexagonal lattices to bend inward (+60o disclinations) and outward (−60o disclinations). Therefore, the CS is composed of curling NGFs (size = 2–10 nm) of unclosed shells with random twisting. These NGFs accumulated over quasi-icosahedral shells form a sphere shape and possess an interlayer distance of ~0.34 nm corresponding to the interplanar distance of graphite (002). Deshmukh et al. also reported the formation of carbon spheres from carbon chains/layers/flakes perpendicular or parallel to the icosahedral shells with size 2–10 nm during the pyrolysis of the carbon precursor [19]. Fig. 2 (b) gives the clear surface analysis of CS using TEM and Fig. 2 (c) shows the clear HR-TEM image of the random twisted unclosed NGFs near the CS surface. It was understood these weakly adhered unclosed NGFs exfoliate from the CS surface during the exfoliation process exhibiting PL properties in organic solvent.

A detailed characterization of the CS collected after CVD synthesis revealed its amorphous nature with smooth spheroidal morphology spheres of the size range from 200 to 350 nm shown in Fig. 2(a). The two broad diffraction rings at (002) and (100) obtained in the SAED pattern shown in the inset of Fig. 2 (c) validate the amorphous nature of CS. It was understood that during the manual shaking, some significant shifts occurred in the organic solvent that resulted in the color change. Fig. 2(a) shows the SEM image of the CS synthesized from CVD with sphere size ranging from 200 to 350 nm.

Fig. 3 (a-b) depicts the SEM image of the black residue after the centrifuging process with a significant size reduction to 20–60 nm. It was observed that a significant amount of the carbon spheres (200–350 nm) in organic solvent had undergone size reduction and some amount of the CS unreacted with the organic solvents might be because of the solvent saturation. It can be inferred that during the manual shaking, an exfoliation process on the surface of CS with NGFs (2–10 nm) occurred that resulted in a size reduction as shown in the SEM images. The majority of the CS dispersed in the organic solvent appeared to have undergone exfoliation, and the size of the CS was significantly reduced. The formation mechanism explained above infers that the flake-like structures on the CS surface exfoliated and the quasi-icosahedral nano shells remained.

Researchers have extensively reported on the liquid phase exfoliation of solid materials. The addition of surfactant, organic salts, water-based cosolvents, and different organic solvents was used as an excellent exfoliation medium. In LPE the solvent plays an important role that determines the overall exfoliation efficiency. The selection of an ideal solvent significantly overcomes the weak van der Waal forces between the layers to exfoliate [20]. In the presence of a suitable liquid solvent, the interlayer interactions of layers are reduced which results in exfoliation of the layers. For the most effective LPE of graphite, the solvents should possess a surface tension value of 30–50 mJm−2 [21]. For the exfoliation of graphite, the surface tension of the solvent must match that of graphite. The surface tension values of low boiling point organic solvents: acetone, methanol, ethanol, and isopropyl alcohol range from 20 to 24 mJm−2 at 20 °C, and high boiling point organic solvents: toluene, xylene, styrene, Dimethylformamide (DMF), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and Ethylene Glycol (EG) range from 27 to 50 mJm−2 at 20 °C. In the LPE of NGFs, the surface tension of the solvents has significant importance and because of this reason, NGFs cannot be formed in water due to the high surface tension value, 73 mJm−2at 25 °C. From the mechanism of CS formation and the significance of surface tension of solvents during LPE, it is understood that the stack of randomly twisted NGF has been exfoliated by the interaction of the organic solvent with the graphitic flakes.

Fig. 4 a) shows the UV absorption spectra of the different organic solvents in which NGFs were synthesized. The UV absorption spectra of NGFs in different organic solvents confirm the UV light absorption with absorption peak intensities. Fig. 4 (b to k) gives the PL spectrum of organic solvents at different excitation wavelengths of acetone, methanol, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, ethylene glycol, styrene, xylene, toluene, DMSO, and DMF, respectively. The peak excitation intensity was created at a fixed wavelength, and the PL spectra give optical excitation as a result of the spontaneous emission of light. With time, the PL intensity of the solvent increased, but this did not lead to a redshift of NGFs in an organic solvent. This means that the size of the flakes did not increase, but more exfoliation took place from the CS surface. It has been reported that the increase in particle size can cause a redshift in the PL emission wavelength of CDs [22]. The PL spectrum intensity of the NGFs in acetone, methanol, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, ethylene glycol, styrene, xylene, toluene, DMSO, and DMF range between 470 nm to 500 nm wavelength. Further characterization was performed for NGFs in DMF that showed maximum PL intensity. The PL spectrum of NGFs in DMF shows that (Fig. 4 (k)) at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm, the strongest fluorescence emission for NGFs in DMF was 470 nm. The quantum yield calculated for NGFs in DMF was 22.51 % by considering quinine sulfate as the standard reference. To avoid deviation line absorbance and molar concentration the UV- Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of the NGFs are kept <0.1.

Various mechanisms have been reported on the fluorescent origin of CDs mainly the quantum confinement effect [23,24], surface state [25], self trapped excitons [26], edge defects [27,28], multi emission centers [29], and conjugated structures [30]. However, researchers have not yet concluded a unified mechanism for the fluorescent properties of CDs. The variation in the optical properties of the CDs is influenced by their small size along with the chemical compositions and structure. It has been also proposed that the fluorescence of CDs is due to ternary fluorescent centers which contain edge states, N-related surface states, and core states. The surface state of the CDs is defined as a complicated system with a synergistic hybridization of carbon backbones and connected functional groups within the system [2,31]. The surface state refers to the adhesion of some functional groups and the edge state refers to the atoms at the edge of the carbon backbone. It has been reported that the core π-π* transitions and edge state n-π* transitions in CDs produce blue and green fluorescence, respectively. The different functional groups attached to the edge states are reported as hydroxyl groups (-OH), carbonyl groups (-CO), amino groups (−NH2), carboxyl groups (-COOH), epoxy, ether, and phenyl groups [32–34]. It can be inferred from the optical characteristics of the NGFs that surface oxidation and functional groups attached might be responsible for the excellent fluorescence properties in different solvents. The difference in the functional groups in different organic solvents may have resulted in the different colors shown in Fig. 1. The oxygen related groups in the solvents can create defects that act as active emission sites for the NGFs that in turn enhance the fluorescence property. The amount of surface defects formed is directly proportional to the trapped exitrons for the recombination and the transition from blue, green, and red fluorescence of CDs derived from varied transitions as mentioned above [2,35,36].

Fig. 5 (a) shows the HR-TEM image of the NGFs with flakes-like particles, and Fig. 5 (b) shows the CS nanoparticles of size 20–40 nm in the supernatant after the exfoliation process. The SAED pattern as shown in the inset of Fig. 5 (b) confirms that the graphitic flakes are amorphous. The ends of the graphitic flakes have unpaired sp2 orbitals and can readily absorb hydrogen and oxygen which results in the attachment of functional groups.

Fig. 5 (c-f) shows the XPS of the NGFs in DMF. The XPS survey spectra confirm the presence of Carbon (66.9 %), oxygen (30.8 %), and nitrogen (2.3 %), and no other impurities were detected in the survey spectra. Deconvolution of the C1s gives the prominent peak at 284.8 eV that corresponds to CC or CC. Two shorter peaks at 286.2 eV and 288.2 eV were also detected in C1 that attributed to CN and CO. Deconvolution of N1s gives a peak at 399.5 eV that corresponds to CN. The O1s core level was decomposed into the main peak at 532.3 eV and a short peak at 530.6 eV that corresponds to CO, C-OH, and CO, respectively. This process is generally discussed as the doping process where the nitrogen atoms are doped into the NGFs in different ways such as graphitic carbon, pyridinic-N, and pyrrolic-N. It can be observed that NGFs prepared in DMF exhibit a high percentage of N and pyrrolic N in the full XPS spectra. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum showed a peak at 399.5 eV, which can be attributed to the pyrrolic N(C-N-C) [37]. However, DMF may react with the functional group of the NGFs to form tertiary N, which has a similar binding energy to pyrrolic N. Thus, further peak fitting of the XPS can be used to analyze other peaks of N-doped NGFs such as Npydo and Nq [1]. These functional groups attached to the edges of the NGFs are attributed to excellent PL emissions like CQDs [38]. In the present study, manual shaking was employed. However, high-energy driving forces from ultrasonication or microwave energy can enhance the exfoliation to get a higher concentration of NGFs in organic solvents. It was noticed that the PL intensity increased for the NGFs in organic solvent due to excess exfoliation of the graphitic flakes using high energy driving forces like ultrasonication and microwave methods.

NGFs of 2–10 nm size can be synthesized from CS in organic solvents by a simple manual shaking caused by the exfoliation of NGFs from the CS surface. The functional groups readily attached to the edges of the graphitic flakes exhibit excellent PL properties. The driving force and the solvent's surface tension play significant roles in the exfoliation of NGFs from CS in the organic solvent. It was found that the black residue can be again used for the synthesis of NGFs. The excellent PL property of this material can have potential in bioimaging, biosensing, and optical applications.
Conception and design of the study: Radhika Panickar
Methodology, Investigation, Acquisition of data: Radhika Panickar
Analysis and/or interpretation of data: Radhika Panickar, C.B Sobhan, Sivaji Chakravorti
Original Draft writing and editing of the manuscript: Radhika Panickar
Revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content and supervision: C.B Sobhan, Sivaji Chakravorti
Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published: Radhika Panickar, C.B Sobhan, Sivaji Chakravorti

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Not received substantial contributions from non-authors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From pristine to heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots: an essential review and prospects for future research

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient light-emitting diode of graphene quantum dots fabricated from graphite intercalation compounds

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Song, M.H. Jang, J. Chung, S.H. Jin, B.H. Kim, S.H. Hur, S. Yoo, Y.H. Cho, S. Jeon, Highly Efficient Light-Emitting Diode of Graphene Quantum Dots Fabricated from Graphite Intercalation Compounds, Adv. Opt. Mater. 2 (2014) 1016–1023. doi:10.1002/adom.201400184.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-colour carbon dots: integration of multiple emission centres into single particles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Ju, H. Nie, Z. Liu, H. Xu, M. Li, C. Wu, H. Wang, S.X.A. Zhang, Full-colour carbon dots: Integration of multiple emission centres into single particles, Nanoscale. 9 (2017) 13326–13333. doi:10.1039/c7nr04576j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient edge-functionalization method to tune the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.P. Qi, H. Hu, L. Bao, Z.L. Zhang, B. Tang, Y. Peng, B.S. Wang, D.W. Pang, An efficient edge-functionalization method to tune the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots, Nanoscale. 7 (2015) 5969–5973. doi:10.1039/c5nr00842e.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-color carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Su, Q. Guan, W. Cai, W. Yang, Q. Xu, Y. Guo, L. Zhang, L. Fei, M. Xu, Multi-color carbon dots for white light-emitting diodes, RSC Adv. 9 (2019) 9700–9708. doi:10.1039/c8ra09868a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NMR and fluorescence studies of Cyclodextrin complexes with guest molecules containing both phenyl and Naphthyl units

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.J. Schneider, T. Blatter, S. Simova, NMR and Fluorescence Studies of Cyclodextrin Complexes with Guest Molecules Containing Both Phenyl and Naphthyl Units, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 113 (1991) 1996–2000. doi:10.1021/ja00006a020.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Tian, G. Qiu, Y. Shen, X. Wang, J. Wang, P. Wang, M. Song, J. Li, T. Li, W. Zhuang, X. Du, Carbon Quantum Dots Modulated NiMoP Hollow Nanopetals as Efficient Electrocatalysts for Hydrogen Evolution, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 58 (2019) 14098–14105. doi:10.1021/acs.iecr.9b01899.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: classification, properties, synthesis, characterization, and applications in health care-an updated review (2018–2021)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.D. Mansuriya, Z. Altintas, Carbon dots: Classification, properties, synthesis, characterization, and applications in health care-an updated review (2018–2021), Nanomaterials. 11 (2021). doi:10.3390/nano11102525.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hu, A. Trinchi, P. Atkin, I. Cole, Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light, Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 2970–2974. doi:10.1002/anie.201411004.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, R. Yuan, M. He, G. Hu, J. Jiang, T. Xu, L. Zhou, W. Chen, W. Xiang, X. Liang, Multicolour nitrogen-doped carbon dots: Tunable photoluminescence and sandwich fluorescent glass-based light-emitting diodes, Nanoscale. 9 (2017) 17849–17858. doi:10.1039/c7nr05363k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three colors emission from S,N Co-doped graphene quantum dots for visible light H2 production and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Qu, Z. Sun, M. Zheng, J. Li, Y. Zhang, G. Zhang, H. Zhao, X. Liu, Z. Xie, Three Colors Emission from S,N Co-doped Graphene Quantum Dots for Visible Light H2 Production and Bioimaging, Adv. Opt. Mater. 3 (2015) 360–367. doi:10.1002/adom.201400549.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of group modification at the edges of carbon quantum dots on fluorescent emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tang, J. Zhang, Y. Zhang, Y. Xiao, Y. Shi, Y. Chen, L. Ding, W. Xu, Influence of Group Modification at the Edges of Carbon Quantum Dots on Fluorescent Emission, Nanoscale Res. Lett. 14 (2019) 2–10. doi:10.1186/s11671-019-3079-7.
